questions.questionText: A patient with hyperammonemia has elevated glutamine in CSF and plasma. The biochemical basis for glutamine accumulation is:
questions.optionB: Ammonia is detoxified in the brain primarily by glutamine synthetase (NH3 + glutamate → glutamine, ATP-dependent) — elevated ammonia drives this reaction forward
questions.explanation: Brain ammonia detoxification: Brain lacks a complete urea cycle. Primary defense: GLUTAMINE SYNTHETASE (astrocytes): NH3 + glutamate + ATP → glutamine. In hyperammonemia: (1) Excess glutamine → osmotic swelling of astrocytes → cerebral edema (major cause of death in acute liver failure), (2) Depletes glutamate (excitatory neurotransmitter) and α-KG (TCA cycle intermediate) → energy failure. CSF glutamine is a sensitive marker of hepatic encephalopathy. Treatment: lactulose (converts NH3→NH4+ in colon, trapped and excreted), rifaximin (reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria). Reference: Harper's — Brain Ammonia Detoxification.

questions.questionText: Newborn screening for PKU measures phenylalanine on day 2-3 of life (not at birth) because:
questions.optionA: The test is unreliable in neonates
questions.optionB: Maternal phenylalanine hydroxylase clears fetal Phe in utero — after birth, Phe accumulates only after protein feeding begins, so day 1 samples may give false negatives
questions.optionC: Phenylalanine is not present at birth
questions.explanation: PKU screening timing: In utero, maternal PAH metabolizes fetal phenylalanine crossing the placenta → cord blood Phe is NORMAL even in PKU. After birth: (1) No maternal clearance, (2) Protein feeding (breast milk/formula) provides Phe, (3) Without functional PAH, Phe accumulates. Day 1 sample: may be falsely NORMAL (insufficient time for Phe accumulation). Optimal: 24-72 hours after birth AND after adequate protein intake. Tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS): current method — simultaneously screens for PKU, MSUD, MCAD, and 40+ IEMs from a single dried blood spot. Reference: Harper's — PKU Newborn Screening.

questions.questionText: Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Branched-chain α-keto acid dehydrogenase complex
questions.explanation: MSUD: deficiency of branched-chain α-keto acid dehydrogenase (BCKD) complex → cannot decarboxylate branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, valine — LIV). Accumulate → sweet maple syrup odor in urine. Severe: neonatal encephalopathy, seizures, intellectual disability, death if untreated. Treatment: diet restricted in branched-chain amino acids, thiamine supplementation (cofactor, some thiamine-responsive forms). Reference: Harper's — MSUD.

questions.questionText: Alkaptonuria is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Homogentisic acid oxidase (in tyrosine degradation pathway)
questions.explanation: Alkaptonuria: AR, homogentisic acid oxidase deficiency → homogentisic acid (HGA) accumulates. HGA is excreted in urine (turns BLACK on standing — oxidizes). Ochronosis: bluish-black pigmentation of cartilage, sclerae, ears (HGA polymerizes in connective tissue). Arthritis (degenerative) in large joints. One of Garrod's original 4 'inborn errors of metabolism.' Usually benign course in childhood. Reference: Harper's — Alkaptonuria.

questions.questionText: Homocystinuria is most commonly caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Cystathionine β-synthase (CBS)
questions.explanation: Homocystinuria: most common cause is CBS deficiency → cannot convert homocysteine → cystathionine (requires PLP/B6). Homocysteine accumulates → Marfanoid habitus, ectopia lentis (lens dislocation — downward/inferonasal, unlike Marfan — upward), thromboembolism (leading cause of death), intellectual disability, osteoporosis. Treatment: pyridoxine (B6), folate, B12 (remethylation pathway), betaine, low-methionine diet. Also caused by MTHFR or B12 deficiency. Reference: Harper's — Homocystinuria.

questions.questionText: A patient on anti-TB therapy with isoniazid develops sideroblastic anemia. The biochemical basis is that PLP (pyridoxal phosphate) is required by:
questions.optionB: ALA synthase — the rate-limiting enzyme of heme synthesis; INH depletes PLP → impaired ALA synthesis → defective heme → iron accumulates in mitochondria forming ringed sideroblasts
questions.explanation: PLP-dependent enzymes and sideroblastic anemia: ALA synthase (ALAS2, erythroid form) catalyzes: glycine + succinyl-CoA → δ-aminolevulinic acid (ALA). This is the COMMITTED, RATE-LIMITING step of heme synthesis. ALAS2 requires PLP (B6) as cofactor. INH depletes PLP → impaired ALA synthesis → heme production decreased → iron continues to enter mitochondria but cannot be incorporated into protoporphyrin IX → iron deposits in mitochondria form a ring around the nucleus = RINGED SIDEROBLASTS. Other PLP-dependent enzymes: transaminases (ALT, AST), decarboxylases (GAD, DOPA decarboxylase), cystathionine β-synthase, glycogen phosphorylase, serine hydroxymethyltransferase. Reference: Harper's — PLP and Heme Synthesis.

questions.questionText: N-acetylglutamate (NAG) is an essential activator of CPS-I. NAG is synthesized by NAG synthase from acetyl-CoA and glutamate. In which clinical scenario does NAG deficiency mimic a urea cycle defect?
questions.optionA: Organic acidemias where toxic metabolites inhibit NAG synthase
questions.optionB: BOTH primary NAG synthase deficiency AND organic acidemias (propionic/methylmalonic acidemia) where accumulated CoA derivatives inhibit NAG synthase → secondary CPS-I inactivation → hyperammonemia
questions.explanation: NAG and CPS-I activation: CPS-I (mitochondrial, urea cycle) is ALLOSTERICALLY ACTIVATED by N-acetylglutamate (NAG) — without NAG, CPS-I is virtually inactive. NAG synthase: acetyl-CoA + glutamate → NAG. Activated by arginine (feed-forward regulation). Primary NAG synthase deficiency: rare autosomal recessive → hyperammonemia identical to CPS-I deficiency. Treatment: carglumic acid (synthetic NAG analog — directly activates CPS-I). Secondary NAG deficiency: organic acidemias (propionic, methylmalonic) → propionyl-CoA and methylmalonyl-CoA accumulate → INHIBIT NAG synthase → reduced NAG → CPS-I inactive → hyperammonemia. This explains why hyperammonemia occurs in organic acidemias despite intact urea cycle enzymes. Reference: Harper's — NAG Synthase.
questions.conceptTag: NAG synthase CPS-I activation organic acidemia

questions.questionText: Hyperammonemia leads to encephalopathy because excess ammonia:
questions.optionB: Depletes α-ketoglutarate (and glutamate) from the TCA cycle, also produces excess glutamine → brain edema
questions.explanation: Hyperammonemia → encephalopathy mechanism: (1) NH3 + α-KG → glutamate (glutamate dehydrogenase) → depletes α-KG from TCA cycle → impairs energy production. (2) Glutamate + NH3 → glutamine (glutamine synthetase in astrocytes) → osmotic effect → cerebral edema. (3) NH3 also interferes with neurotransmitter balance and impairs excitatory neurotransmission. Causes: urea cycle enzyme deficiencies, liver failure. Treatment: lactulose, rifaximin, protein restriction, sodium benzoate/phenylbutyrate. Reference: Harper's — Hyperammonemia.

questions.questionText: Cystinuria is a disorder of:
questions.optionB: Renal tubular transport of dibasic amino acids (cystine, ornithine, arginine, lysine — COAL)
questions.explanation: Cystinuria: AR defect in renal tubular (and intestinal) transport of dibasic amino acids — Cystine, Ornithine, Arginine, Lysine (mnemonic: COAL). Cystine is poorly soluble → precipitates in urine → hexagonal cystine kidney stones (most common cause of stones in children). Positive cyanide-nitroprusside test. Treatment: high fluid intake, urine alkalinization (>pH 7.5), penicillamine (chelates cystine → soluble disulfide). NOT a metabolic error but a transport defect. Reference: Harper's — Cystinuria.

questions.questionText: S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) is the universal methyl donor and is synthesized from:
questions.optionB: Methionine (+ ATP)
questions.explanation: SAM: synthesized from methionine + ATP (by methionine adenosyltransferase/SAM synthetase). SAM donates methyl groups for: DNA methylation (epigenetics), creatine synthesis, phospholipid synthesis (PC from PE), catecholamine metabolism (norepinephrine → epinephrine by PNMT), melatonin, carnitine. After donating CH3: SAM → S-adenosylhomocysteine → homocysteine → remethylated to methionine (requires B12 and folate) or transsulfurated to cysteine (requires B6). Reference: Harper's — SAM.

questions.questionText: Hartnup disease involves defective transport of:
questions.optionB: Neutral amino acids (especially tryptophan) in the intestine and kidney
questions.explanation: Hartnup disease: AR defect in neutral amino acid transporter (SLC6A19) in intestinal and renal tubular epithelium. Tryptophan malabsorption → decreased NAD+ (nicotinamide) synthesis → pellagra-like symptoms (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia — the 3Ds). Tryptophan → nicotinamide → NAD+. Also: cerebellar ataxia, psychiatric symptoms. Aminoaciduria. Treatment: nicotinamide (niacin) supplementation, high-protein diet. Usually mild and self-limiting. Reference: Harper's — Hartnup Disease.

questions.questionText: In OTC deficiency, orotic acid is elevated in urine. This occurs because:
questions.optionB: Carbamoyl phosphate (accumulated due to blocked urea cycle) spills into the cytoplasm and enters the PYRIMIDINE synthesis pathway → converted to orotic acid
questions.optionC: Orotic acid is a byproduct of ammonia detoxification
questions.optionD: Urea is converted to orotic acid
questions.explanation: OTC deficiency — orotic aciduria mechanism: OTC (ornithine transcarbamylase, X-linked): carbamoyl phosphate + ornithine → citrulline (mitochondrial, urea cycle). Deficiency → carbamoyl phosphate ACCUMULATES in mitochondria → leaks to CYTOPLASM → feeds into PYRIMIDINE synthesis pathway: carbamoyl phosphate + aspartate → carbamoyl aspartate (ATCase) → dihydroorotate → OROTATE → OMP → UMP. Result: orotic aciduria + hyperammonemia. KEY DIFFERENTIAL: CPS-I deficiency also causes hyperammonemia but WITHOUT orotic aciduria (no excess carbamoyl phosphate to spill over). Orotic aciduria helps distinguish OTC from CPS-I deficiency. Hereditary orotic aciduria (UMP synthase deficiency): orotic aciduria WITHOUT hyperammonemia. Reference: Harper's — OTC Orotic Aciduria.
questions.conceptTag: OTC orotic acid carbamoyl phosphate spillover

questions.questionText: Tyrosinemia Type I is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (FAH)
questions.explanation: Tyrosinemia Type I (hepatorenal): FAH deficiency → accumulation of fumarylacetoacetate and succinylacetone → liver failure, renal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome), acute porphyria-like crises, hepatocellular carcinoma risk. Cabbage-like odor. Treatment: NTBC (nitisinone — inhibits 4-HPP dioxygenase upstream, preventing toxic metabolite formation) + low-Tyr/Phe diet. Type II (Richner-Hanhart): TAT deficiency → skin/eye. Type III: 4-HPP dioxygenase → neurological. Reference: Harper's — Tyrosinemia.

questions.questionText: Glycine is a precursor for synthesis of all EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Purines
questions.explanation: Glycine is a precursor for: (1) Heme/porphyrin synthesis (glycine + succinyl-CoA → ALA by ALA synthase), (2) Purine nucleotides (C4, C5, N7 of purine ring from glycine), (3) Creatine (glycine + arginine + SAM), (4) Glutathione (γ-glu-cys-gly), (5) Bile acid conjugation (glycocholic acid), (6) Hippuric acid (glycine + benzoic acid). Cholesterol is NOT derived from glycine — it is derived from acetyl-CoA (via mevalonate pathway). Reference: Harper's — Glycine Metabolism.

questions.questionText: OTC (ornithine transcarbamylase) deficiency is the most common urea cycle disorder and its inheritance is:
questions.optionB: X-linked (most common inherited urea cycle defect)
questions.explanation: OTC deficiency: X-linked recessive (ONLY urea cycle enzyme deficiency that is X-linked — all others are AR). Most common inherited urea cycle defect. Males severely affected (neonatal hyperammonemia → encephalopathy). Females may be carriers with variable expression. OTC: mitochondrial enzyme, catalyzes carbamoyl phosphate + ornithine → citrulline. Diagnostic: elevated orotic acid in urine (carbamoyl phosphate diverted to pyrimidine synthesis pathway). Reference: Harper's — OTC Deficiency.

questions.questionText: The immediate nitrogen donor in most transamination reactions is eventually collected as:
questions.optionB: Glutamate
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Amino groups are funneled to glutamate via transamination; glutamate can undergo oxidative deamination to release free ammonia for urea cycle.

questions.questionText: Hyperammonemia primarily causes neurotoxicity because excess ammonia in brain drives glutamate toward:
questions.optionB: Glutamine
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Ammonia detoxification in astrocytes forms glutamine from glutamate, potentially depleting neurotransmitter pools and contributing to cerebral edema.

questions.questionText: The methyl donor formed from methionine and ATP in one-carbon metabolism is:
questions.optionB: S-adenosylmethionine (SAM)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. SAM is the universal methyl donor for DNA/RNA/protein/lipid methylation reactions, linking methionine cycle with folate-B12 pathways.

questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme in heme biosynthesis is:
questions.optionB: ALA synthase (δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase — requires PLP, condensation of glycine + succinyl-CoA)
questions.explanation: ALA synthase: rate-limiting step of heme biosynthesis. Occurs in mitochondria. Glycine + succinyl-CoA → δ-aminolevulinic acid (ALA). Cofactor: PLP (pyridoxal phosphate, vitamin B6). Two isoforms: ALAS1 (liver, ubiquitous — regulated by heme feedback) and ALAS2 (erythroid — regulated by iron/erythropoietin). Lead poisoning inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase (NOT ALA synthase). Drugs inducing ALAS1 (barbiturates, alcohol) can precipitate acute porphyria attacks. Reference: Harper's — Heme Biosynthesis.

questions.questionText: Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Porphobilinogen deaminase (also known as hydroxymethylbilane synthase/uroporphyrinogen I synthase)
questions.explanation: AIP: autosomal dominant, PBG deaminase (HMB synthase) deficiency → accumulation of ALA and PBG (NOT porphyrins — hence NO photosensitivity). Presents with: acute abdominal pain (most common — the 5 P's: Pain, Polyneuropathy, Psychological symptoms, Port wine-colored urine, Precipitation by drugs). Triggered by: barbiturates, alcohol, oral contraceptives, fasting (all induce ALAS1). Diagnosis: elevated urine ALA and PBG. Watson-Schwartz test positive. Treatment: IV hemin (feedback inhibits ALAS1), glucose infusion. Reference: Harper's — AIP.

questions.questionText: S-Adenosylmethionine (SAM) is the universal methyl donor synthesized from:
questions.optionB: Methionine and ATP (by methionine adenosyltransferase/MAT)
questions.explanation: SAM synthesis: methionine + ATP → SAM (by methionine adenosyltransferase/MAT). SAM donates methyl group → S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) → homocysteine. Homocysteine can be: (1) remethylated to methionine by methionine synthase (requires B12 + N5-methyl-THF) or BHMT (betaine), (2) transsulfurated to cysteine by cystathionine β-synthase (requires B6). SAM methylates: DNA (epigenetics), norepinephrine → epinephrine (PNMT), phosphatidylethanolamine → phosphatidylcholine, creatine synthesis. Reference: Harper's — SAM Cycle.

questions.questionText: Tetrahydrofolate (THF) serves as a carrier of:
questions.optionB: One-carbon units (methyl, methylene, methenyl, formyl, formimino)
questions.explanation: THF: active form of folic acid (vitamin B9). Carries one-carbon (1C) units at different oxidation states: N5-methyl-THF (most reduced — methyl group for methionine synthesis via B12), N5,N10-methylene-THF (for dTMP synthesis by thymidylate synthase), N10-formyl-THF (for purine ring synthesis), N5-formimino-THF (histidine degradation). DHFR (dihydrofolate reductase) regenerates THF from DHF — target of methotrexate. Folate deficiency → megaloblastic anemia (impaired DNA synthesis). Reference: Harper's — One-Carbon Metabolism.

questions.questionText: A patient with carcinoid syndrome has pellagra-like symptoms despite adequate dietary niacin. The biochemical explanation is:
questions.optionA: Carcinoid tumors destroy niacin in the blood
questions.optionB: Carcinoid tumors divert >60% of dietary tryptophan toward serotonin synthesis (via tryptophan hydroxylase), depleting the pool available for niacin (NAD+/NADP+) synthesis
questions.optionC: Increased niacin excretion by the kidneys
questions.optionD: Malabsorption of niacin from the gut
questions.explanation: Carcinoid syndrome and niacin deficiency: Normally ~1% of dietary tryptophan is converted to serotonin. Carcinoid tumors overexpress tryptophan hydroxylase → massively increased serotonin production → up to 60% of tryptophan diverted → depletes tryptophan available for NAD+/NADP+ synthesis pathway (tryptophan → N-formylkynurenine → ... → NAD+). 60 mg tryptophan = 1 mg niacin equivalent. Result: functional niacin deficiency → pellagra-like symptoms (4 Ds: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, death). Diagnosis: elevated urinary 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid, serotonin metabolite). Hartnup disease: similar pellagra mechanism — impaired neutral amino acid (including tryptophan) absorption. Reference: Harper's — Carcinoid Tryptophan.

questions.questionText: Histamine intolerance results from deficiency of which enzyme responsible for histamine catabolism?
questions.optionB: Diamine oxidase (DAO) — the primary enzyme for extracellular histamine degradation in the gut; deficiency leads to accumulation after histamine-rich foods
questions.explanation: Histamine catabolism: Two pathways: (1) DIAMINE OXIDASE (DAO/AOC1): primarily in intestinal mucosa, kidney, placenta. Oxidatively deaminates histamine → imidazole acetaldehyde. Principal route for EXTRACELLULAR/dietary histamine degradation. (2) Histamine N-methyltransferase (HNMT): intracellular, uses SAM → N-methylhistamine → MAO → N-methylimidazole acetic acid. DAO deficiency/inhibition: histamine from diet (fermented foods, aged cheese, wine, fish) not adequately degraded → histamine accumulation → symptoms mimicking allergy: headache, flushing, urticaria, GI symptoms, hypotension. NOT true allergy (no IgE). Drugs inhibiting DAO: NSAIDs, some antibiotics. Pregnancy: DAO levels increase 500-fold (placental production) — may explain remission of allergies during pregnancy. Reference: Harper's — Histamine Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (most common porphyria)
questions.explanation: PCT: most common porphyria. Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency → accumulation of uroporphyrins and heptacarboxyl porphyrins. Presentation: photosensitivity (porphyrins absorb light → generate ROS → skin damage), blistering skin lesions on sun-exposed areas, hypertrichosis (facial), hyperpigmentation, hepatic siderosis. NO abdominal pain or neuropsychiatric symptoms (unlike AIP). Associated with: alcohol, hepatitis C, estrogens, iron overload, HIV. Treatment: phlebotomy (reduces iron), low-dose antimalarials (chloroquine). Reference: Harper's — PCT.

questions.questionText: The 'methyl folate trap' hypothesis explains megaloblastic anemia in B12 deficiency because:
questions.optionA: Folate is destroyed by B12 absence
questions.optionB: B12 deficiency traps folate as N5-methyl-THF (cannot be converted back to THF) → functional folate deficiency
questions.optionD: Folate and B12 have identical functions
questions.explanation: Methyl folate trap: B12 is required by methionine synthase to convert homocysteine → methionine. This reaction also converts N5-methyl-THF → THF. Without B12: N5-methyl-THF accumulates (trapped) → THF cannot be regenerated → N5,N10-methylene-THF not available → dTMP synthesis impaired → DNA synthesis affected → megaloblastic anemia. This explains why B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia identical to folate deficiency (functional folate deficiency). Folate supplementation may correct anemia but NOT the neurological damage of B12 deficiency. Reference: Harper's — Methyl Folate Trap.

questions.questionText: Catecholamines (dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine) are synthesized from:
questions.optionB: Tyrosine (rate-limiting step: tyrosine hydroxylase, requires BH4)
questions.explanation: Catecholamine synthesis from tyrosine: (1) Tyrosine → DOPA (tyrosine hydroxylase, BH4 — rate-limiting), (2) DOPA → dopamine (DOPA decarboxylase, PLP), (3) Dopamine → norepinephrine (dopamine β-hydroxylase, vitamin C, copper), (4) NE → epinephrine (PNMT/phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase, SAM — found only in adrenal medulla). Parkinson's disease: loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra. Treatment: L-DOPA (crosses BBB, unlike dopamine). Pheochromocytoma: excess catecholamines from adrenal medulla. Reference: Harper's — Catecholamine Synthesis.

questions.questionText: GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid) is synthesized from glutamate by:
questions.optionB: Glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD, requires PLP)
questions.explanation: GABA: major inhibitory neurotransmitter in CNS. Synthesized by decarboxylation of glutamate (by GAD, PLP-dependent). GAD autoantibodies: found in Type 1 diabetes mellitus and stiff-person syndrome. GABA acts on: GABAA receptors (ligand-gated Cl- channels — target of benzodiazepines, barbiturates, alcohol) and GABAB receptors (G-protein coupled — target of baclofen). GABA is degraded by GABA transaminase → succinate semialdehyde → succinic acid (enters TCA cycle). Vigabatrin: irreversible GABA-T inhibitor (antiepileptic). Reference: Harper's — GABA Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Lead poisoning inhibits which two enzymes in heme synthesis?
questions.optionB: ALA dehydratase (porphobilinogen synthase) and ferrochelatase
questions.explanation: Lead (Pb) inhibits: (1) ALA dehydratase (zinc-dependent) → ALA accumulates in urine, (2) Ferrochelatase (inserts Fe2+ into protoporphyrin IX to form heme) → free erythrocyte protoporphyrin (FEP) and zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP) accumulate. Clinical: microcytic hypochromic anemia (impaired heme → impaired hemoglobin synthesis), basophilic stippling of RBCs, lead line on gums (Burton line), wrist/foot drop, encephalopathy (children), renal tubular damage. ALA synthase is NOT inhibited (actually induced due to decreased heme feedback). Reference: Harper's — Lead and Heme.

questions.questionText: Creatine is synthesized from which three amino acids?
questions.optionB: Glycine, arginine, and methionine (SAM provides methyl group)
questions.explanation: Creatine synthesis: Step 1 (kidney): glycine + arginine → guanidinoacetate (by AGAT/amidinotransferase). Step 2 (liver): guanidinoacetate + SAM → creatine (by GAMT/guanidinoacetate methyltransferase). Creatine → phosphocreatine (by creatine kinase, using ATP) → serves as immediate energy reserve in muscle and brain. Spontaneous cyclization of phosphocreatine/creatine → creatinine (excreted by kidneys — used to estimate GFR). Serum creatinine is the most common test for kidney function. Reference: Harper's — Creatine Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Branched-chain α-keto acid dehydrogenase complex (BCKDH — affects leucine, isoleucine, valine)
questions.explanation: MSUD: deficiency of branched-chain α-keto acid dehydrogenase (BCKDH) complex → accumulation of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, valine) and their α-keto acids. Leucine and its α-keto acid (α-ketoisocaproate) are most toxic (neurotoxic). Features: sweet maple syrup odor of urine/body, vomiting, seizures, encephalopathy, intellectual disability. Onset within first week of life. BCKDH is structurally similar to PDH and α-KGDH (all use TPP, lipoic acid, CoA, FAD, NAD+). Some thiamine-responsive forms exist. Reference: Harper's — MSUD.

questions.questionText: Nitric oxide (NO) is synthesized from which amino acid?
questions.optionB: Arginine (by NO synthase — NOS, requires BH4, NADPH)
questions.explanation: NO synthesis: arginine → citrulline + NO (by nitric oxide synthase/NOS). Cofactors: BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin), NADPH, FAD, FMN, heme. Three NOS isoforms: nNOS (neuronal — neurotransmitter), eNOS (endothelial — vasodilation), iNOS (inducible/macrophage — immune defense). NO activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → vasodilation. Nitroglycerin/SNP release NO → used in angina/hypertensive crisis. Sildenafil inhibits PDE5 (prevents cGMP degradation) → potentiates NO effect. NO overproduction → septic shock. Reference: Harper's — Nitric Oxide.

questions.questionText: Asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA) is a cardiovascular risk biomarker because it:
questions.optionB: Is an endogenous competitive inhibitor of all NOS isoforms → reduces NO synthesis → endothelial dysfunction, vasoconstriction, and increased cardiovascular risk
questions.explanation: ADMA and cardiovascular disease: NO synthesis: L-arginine → L-citrulline + NO (by NOS — eNOS, nNOS, iNOS). ADMA (asymmetric dimethylarginine): formed by methylation of arginine residues in proteins (by PRMTs/protein arginine methyltransferases using SAM) → released on proteolysis. ADMA is a COMPETITIVE INHIBITOR of all NOS isoforms → blocks NO production → endothelial dysfunction → vasoconstriction, platelet aggregation, leukocyte adhesion, smooth muscle proliferation. Elevated ADMA is an independent cardiovascular risk factor (associated with atherosclerosis, heart failure, CKD, preeclampsia, diabetes). ADMA metabolism: degraded by DDAH (dimethylarginine dimethylaminohydrolase) → citrulline + dimethylamine. SDMA (symmetric dimethylarginine): NOT a NOS inhibitor, cleared renally (GFR marker). Reference: Harper's — ADMA.

questions.questionText: In alkaptonuria, the darkening of urine on standing and ochronosis (pigment deposition in cartilage) are due to:
questions.optionB: Homogentisic acid (HGA) accumulation → auto-oxidation and polymerization to a melanin-like pigment (benzoquinone acetic acid polymer) that deposits in connective tissue
questions.explanation: Alkaptonuria pathophysiology: HGA oxidase deficiency → HGA accumulates. HGA is initially colorless but undergoes: (1) AUTO-OXIDATION in alkaline conditions → benzoquinone acetic acid (dark pigment), (2) POLYMERIZATION → melanin-like pigment. Urine: turns dark brown/black on standing (alkali/air exposure accelerates oxidation — hence 'alkaptonuria'). Ochronosis: pigment deposits in cartilage, tendons, sclera, heart valves → bluish-black discoloration. Over years → degenerative arthropathy (especially spine, large joints). Diagnosis: urine darkens on adding NaOH, ferric chloride test positive. Nitisinone (NTBC): reduces HGA production by inhibiting 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (upstream). Same drug used in tyrosinemia type I. Reference: Harper's — Alkaptonuria.

questions.questionText: Melanin is synthesized from tyrosine by the enzyme:
questions.optionB: Tyrosinase (copper-dependent enzyme in melanocytes)
questions.explanation: Melanin synthesis (melanogenesis): Tyrosine → DOPA → dopaquinone (by TYROSINASE, copper-dependent, in melanosomes within melanocytes). Dopaquinone → eumelanin (brown/black) or pheomelanin (red/yellow). Albinism: Type 1 (oculocutaneous): tyrosinase deficiency → absent melanin in skin, hair, eyes → white hair, pale skin, pink eyes, nystagmus, photophobia. Tyrosine HYDROXYLASE (BH4-dependent) is for catecholamine synthesis — different enzyme! Reference: Harper's — Melanin Biosynthesis.

questions.questionText: A patient with oculocutaneous albinism type 1A has completely absent melanin. The defect is in:
questions.optionB: Tyrosinase — the rate-limiting copper-dependent enzyme that catalyzes both tyrosine → DOPA and DOPA → dopaquinone in the melanin synthesis pathway
questions.explanation: Melanin synthesis and OCA1A: Melanin biosynthesis (in melanosomes of melanocytes): Tyrosine → DOPA (tyrosinase, rate-limiting) → dopaquinone (tyrosinase) → further polymerization. Tyrosinase: copper-dependent enzyme (explains why copper deficiency/Menkes → hypopigmentation). OCA1A: complete tyrosinase absence → NO melanin → white hair, white skin, pink-red iris throughout life. OCA1B (yellow albinism): partial tyrosinase activity → some melanin accumulates with age. OCA2: most common worldwide — P protein (melanosomal pH regulation) defect. OCA3: TYRP1 deficiency, OCA4: SLC45A2. Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome: OCA + platelet storage pool deficiency (ceroid deposits) + pulmonary fibrosis. All OCA types: increased skin cancer risk (decreased UV protection). Reference: Harper's — Melanin Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Glutamate dehydrogenase is unique among amino acid metabolizing enzymes because it:
questions.optionB: Can use either NAD+ or NADP+ as coenzyme and is the only enzyme that performs oxidative deamination of amino acids
questions.explanation: Glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH): mitochondrial enzyme, catalyzes reversible oxidative deamination of glutamate → α-ketoglutarate + NH4+ (using NAD+ or NADP+). This is the ONLY amino acid that undergoes significant direct oxidative deamination. Direction depends on energy state: high ATP/GTP inhibits (favors glutamate synthesis → nitrogen storage), low energy activates (favors deamination → feeds α-KG to TCA cycle). Most other amino acids are first transaminated to glutamate (by aminotransferases/PLP-dependent), then glutamate is deaminated by GDH. Reference: Harper's — GDH.

questions.questionText: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria (CEP/Günther disease) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Uroporphyrinogen III synthase (cosynthase)
questions.explanation: CEP (Günther disease): rarest porphyria, autosomal recessive. Uroporphyrinogen III synthase deficiency → non-enzymatic uroporphyrinogen I accumulates (instead of uroporphyrinogen III) → severely photosensitive. Features: severe cutaneous photosensitivity from infancy, red-brown teeth (erythrodontia — fluorescing under UV), hemolytic anemia, splenomegaly, red urine (from birth — pink-staining diapers). The 'werewolf disease' — historical basis for vampirism/werewolf myths. Treatment: sun avoidance, transfusions, splenectomy, bone marrow transplant. Reference: Harper's — CEP.

questions.questionText: A newborn with PKU phenotype (elevated phenylalanine) fails to improve on a low-phenylalanine diet and develops progressive neurological deterioration. The most likely cause is:
questions.optionB: Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) deficiency — malignant/atypical PKU affecting neurotransmitter synthesis
questions.explanation: BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin) deficiency — malignant PKU: accounts for ~2% of hyperphenylalaninemias. BH4 is the essential cofactor for: (1) Phenylalanine hydroxylase (Phe → Tyr), (2) Tyrosine hydroxylase (Tyr → DOPA — dopamine synthesis), (3) Tryptophan hydroxylase (Trp → 5-HTP — serotonin synthesis), (4) NOS (nitric oxide synthesis). Without BH4: phenylalanine elevated (like classic PKU) BUT also neurotransmitter synthesis impaired → severe progressive neurological deterioration DESPITE dietary Phe restriction. Treatment: BH4 supplementation + L-DOPA/carbidopa (dopamine precursor) + 5-hydroxytryptophan (serotonin precursor) + folinic acid. BH4 is regenerated by dihydropteridine reductase (DHPR) — DHPR deficiency also causes this phenotype. Reference: Harper's — BH4 Deficiency.

questions.questionText: A child presents with abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and elevated urinary δ-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) with NORMAL porphobilinogen (PBG). Which condition should be considered alongside AIP?
questions.optionB: Lead poisoning (inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase → elevated ALA with normal/low PBG)
questions.explanation: Lead poisoning vs AIP — critical differential: Both present with abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and elevated urinary ALA. KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURE: Lead poisoning: ALA elevated, PBG NORMAL (because lead inhibits ALA dehydratase → ALA accumulates BEFORE PBG formation step). Also inhibits ferrochelatase → protoporphyrin IX accumulates in RBCs (zinc protoporphyrin). AIP (acute intermittent porphyria): ALA AND PBG BOTH elevated (PBG deaminase/HMBS deficiency → PBG accumulates along with its precursor ALA). Other lead poisoning features: basophilic stippling of RBCs (impaired pyrimidine-5'-nucleotidase), Burton's line (blue-black line on gums), wrist/foot drop, sideroblastic anemia, learning difficulties in children. Treatment: chelation (succimer for children, EDTA/dimercaprol for adults). Reference: Harper's — Lead vs AIP.
questions.conceptTag: Lead poisoning vs AIP ALA differential

questions.questionText: Cystinuria and cystinosis are both associated with cystine but differ fundamentally because:
questions.optionA: Both are amino acid metabolism defects
questions.optionB: Cystinuria is a renal/intestinal transport defect (dibasic amino acids) causing kidney stones; cystinosis is a lysosomal cystine transport defect (cystinosin) causing systemic crystal accumulation
questions.optionC: Cystinuria causes liver failure while cystinosis is benign
questions.optionD: Both exclusively cause renal failure by the same mechanism
questions.explanation: Cystinuria: autosomal recessive defect in SLC3A1/SLC7A9 → impaired renal/intestinal transport of dibasic amino acids (cystine, ornithine, arginine, lysine — mnemonic: COAL). Cystine is poorly soluble → precipitates in urine → hexagonal cystine kidney stones (pathognomonic crystals). NOT an amino acid metabolism defect — transport only. Treatment: high fluid intake, urine alkalinization, penicillamine (chelates cystine). Cystinosis: autosomal recessive CTNS gene → defective cystinosin (lysosomal cystine transporter) → cystine ACCUMULATES INSIDE LYSOSOMES of ALL cells → corneal crystals (pathognomonic), renal Fanconi syndrome, progressive renal failure, hypothyroidism, growth retardation. Treatment: cysteamine (enters lysosomes, reduces cystine). Reference: Harper's — Cystinuria vs Cystinosis.

questions.questionText: Non-ketotic hyperglycinemia (NKH/glycine encephalopathy) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Glycine cleavage system (GCS — a mitochondrial multienzyme complex that degrades glycine)
questions.explanation: Non-ketotic hyperglycinemia (NKH): autosomal recessive deficiency of glycine cleavage system (GCS) — mitochondrial multienzyme complex in liver, brain, kidney. GCS components: P-protein (glycine decarboxylase — most commonly mutated), H-protein, T-protein, L-protein. Normally: glycine + THF + NAD+ → CO2 + NH3 + N5,N10-methylene-THF + NADH. Deficiency → glycine accumulates in plasma and CSF (CSF/plasma glycine ratio > 0.08 is diagnostic — normally < 0.02). Glycine is an inhibitory neurotransmitter (glycine receptor) AND excitatory co-agonist at NMDA receptors. Features: severe neonatal seizures, hypotonia, apnea, hiccups, intellectual disability. NON-KETOTIC distinguishes from organic acidemias with secondary hyperglycinemia (which are ketotic). Treatment: sodium benzoate (conjugates glycine), NMDA antagonists (dextromethorphan). Reference: Harper's — NKH.

questions.questionText: In carcinoid syndrome, niacin deficiency (pellagra-like symptoms) can occur because:
questions.optionA: The tumor blocks intestinal niacin absorption
questions.optionB: The carcinoid tumor diverts tryptophan to excessive serotonin synthesis → less tryptophan available for niacin (NAD+/NADP+) synthesis
questions.optionC: Niacin is directly destroyed by serotonin
questions.explanation: Carcinoid syndrome and niacin deficiency — metabolic integration: Carcinoid tumors (neuroendocrine tumors, mainly ileum) produce excessive serotonin (5-HT). Tryptophan → 5-hydroxytryptophan (tryptophan hydroxylase) → serotonin (DOPA decarboxylase). Large carcinoid tumors may divert up to 60% of dietary tryptophan to serotonin synthesis → insufficient tryptophan remains for the kynurenine pathway (normally: tryptophan → NAD+/NADP+ via kynurenine intermediates; 60 mg Trp → 1 mg niacin). Result: functional niacin deficiency → pellagra-like symptoms (dermatitis, diarrhea). Carcinoid syndrome features: flushing, diarrhea, bronchospasm, right-sided heart disease (tricuspid/pulmonary valve fibrosis — serotonin-mediated). Diagnosis: elevated urinary 5-HIAA. Reference: Harper's — Carcinoid and Tryptophan.

questions.questionText: MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase) C677T polymorphism is clinically significant because it leads to:
questions.optionB: Elevated plasma homocysteine (mild hyperhomocysteinemia) → increased risk of NTDs, cardiovascular disease, and venous thromboembolism
questions.explanation: MTHFR C677T polymorphism: common (5-15% homozygous TT in many populations). MTHFR catalyzes: N5,N10-methylene-THF → N5-methyl-THF (irreversible). N5-methyl-THF is the methyl donor for homocysteine → methionine (via methionine synthase, B12-dependent). C677T mutation → thermolabile MTHFR with ~30% reduced activity (TT homozygotes) → less N5-methyl-THF → impaired homocysteine remethylation → mild hyperhomocysteinemia (especially if folate status is low). Consequences: increased risk of neural tube defects (NTDs), cardiovascular disease, venous thrombosis, pregnancy complications (recurrent miscarriage, preeclampsia). Management: adequate folate supplementation (compensates for reduced MTHFR activity). NOT an indication for folic acid megadoses. Reference: Harper's — MTHFR Polymorphism.

questions.questionText: A neonate with hyperammonemia has LOW plasma citrulline and HIGH urinary orotic acid. The most likely urea cycle defect is:
questions.optionB: Ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency
questions.explanation: Urea cycle defect differentiation by metabolite pattern — high-yield: OTC deficiency: LOW citrulline (OTC makes citrulline from carbamoyl phosphate + ornithine) + HIGH orotic acid (carbamoyl phosphate leaks to pyrimidine pathway). X-linked. CPS-I deficiency: LOW citrulline + LOW/NORMAL orotic acid (no excess carbamoyl phosphate produced). Autosomal recessive. Citrullinemia (ASS deficiency): HIGH citrulline (citrulline cannot be converted to argininosuccinate). Argininosuccinate lyase (ASL) deficiency: HIGH citrulline + HIGH argininosuccinate in plasma/urine. Arginase deficiency: HIGH arginine, LEAST severe, progressive spasticity. KEY PRINCIPLE: the metabolite BEFORE the block accumulates, the metabolite AFTER is decreased. Orotic acid distinguishes OTC from CPS-I. Reference: Harper's — Urea Cycle Defect Differentiation.

questions.questionText: Variegate porphyria (VP) is classified as an ACUTE porphyria that uniquely presents with:
questions.optionA: Only neurovisceral symptoms without skin involvement
questions.optionB: BOTH acute neurovisceral attacks (like AIP) AND cutaneous photosensitivity (blistering skin lesions)
questions.optionC: Only cutaneous photosensitivity without acute attacks
questions.explanation: Variegate porphyria (VP): autosomal dominant, protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPO) deficiency. VP is unique because it combines features of BOTH acute and cutaneous porphyrias. Acute symptoms: abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms, autonomic dysfunction (same as AIP — due to ALA/PBG accumulation). Cutaneous symptoms: photosensitivity with skin fragility, blistering, scarring (due to porphyrin accumulation in skin — also seen in PCT). Diagnosis: elevated fecal protoporphyrin (distinguishing feature), elevated urine ALA/PBG during attacks, plasma fluorescence emission peak at ~626 nm. Common in South Africa (Afrikaner population — founder effect). Hereditary coproporphyria (HCP): also mixed (acute + cutaneous) but coproporphyrinogen oxidase deficiency. PCT: cutaneous ONLY. AIP: acute ONLY. Reference: Harper's — Variegate Porphyria.

questions.questionText: Ornithine decarboxylase (ODC, rate-limiting enzyme for polyamine synthesis) converts ornithine to:
questions.optionB: Putrescine (a diamine, the precursor of spermidine and spermine — essential for cell growth and proliferation)
questions.explanation: Polyamine synthesis — from ornithine: ODC (ornithine decarboxylase): ornithine → putrescine (requires PLP). Putrescine → spermidine (spermidine synthase, dcSAM as aminopropyl donor) → spermine (spermine synthase). Polyamines are polycationic molecules essential for: (1) DNA packaging and stabilization (bind negative charges), (2) Cell proliferation (rapidly dividing cells have high polyamine content), (3) Gene expression regulation, (4) Ion channel modulation. ODC has the shortest half-life of any mammalian enzyme (~10-30 min) — highly regulated. ODC is overexpressed in many cancers. DFMO (difluoromethylornithine/eflornithine): irreversible ODC inhibitor — used in African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness) and as antitumor research. dcSAM (decarboxylated SAM): aminopropyl donor for spermidine/spermine synthesis. Reference: Harper's — Polyamine Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Isovaleric acidemia (sweaty feet odor in a sick neonate) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase (leucine degradation pathway)
questions.explanation: Isovaleric acidemia: autosomal recessive deficiency of isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase — the THIRD step in leucine catabolism (after BCKDH). Isovaleryl-CoA accumulates → hydrolyzed to isovaleric acid → characteristic 'sweaty feet' or 'cheesy' odor (very distinctive). Leucine catabolism: leucine → α-ketoisocaproate (transamination) → isovaleryl-CoA (BCKDH) → 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA (isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase — DEFICIENT in this disease) →...→ acetyl-CoA + acetoacetate (leucine is both ketogenic AND glucogenic via acetyl-CoA and acetoacetate). Clinical: neonatal vomiting, lethargy, metabolic acidosis, ketonuria, pancytopenia, odor. Treatment: glycine supplementation (conjugates isovaleric acid → isovalerylglycine, more easily excreted), carnitine, leucine-restricted diet. Reference: Harper's — Isovaleric Acidemia.

questions.questionText: Hartnup disease is caused by a defect in the intestinal and renal transport of:
questions.optionB: Neutral amino acids (including tryptophan) → reduced tryptophan availability → pellagra-like symptoms
questions.explanation: Hartnup disease: autosomal recessive SLC6A19 mutation → defective sodium-dependent neutral amino acid transporter B0AT1 in intestinal brush border and renal proximal tubule → impaired absorption/reabsorption of neutral amino acids (tryptophan, alanine, serine, leucine, isoleucine, valine, phenylalanine, etc.). Reduced tryptophan absorption → decreased niacin synthesis (60 mg Trp → 1 mg niacin via kynurenine pathway) → pellagra-like symptoms: photosensitive rash, diarrhea, cerebellar ataxia, psychiatric symptoms. Aminoaciduria (neutral AAs in urine). Treatment: high-protein diet, nicotinamide supplementation, sun protection. Named after Hartnup family. Cystinuria: transport defect of DIBASIC amino acids (COAL). Reference: Harper's — Hartnup Disease.
questions.conceptTag: Hartnup disease neutral amino acid transport

questions.questionText: Benzoic acid and phenylacetic acid are used therapeutically in urea cycle disorders because they:
questions.optionB: Conjugate with glycine and glutamine respectively → hippuric acid and phenylacetylglutamine → providing alternative nitrogen excretion pathways to bypass the defective urea cycle
questions.explanation: Alternative nitrogen excretion — bypass therapy for urea cycle defects: Sodium benzoate: benzoate + glycine (via glycine-N-acyltransferase) → hippuric acid (excreted in urine). Each mole of hippuric acid removes 1 nitrogen. Glycine pool is replenished from serine (consuming another nitrogen). Net: 2 nitrogens removed per benzoate. Sodium phenylbutyrate: prodrug → phenylacetate (by β-oxidation) + glutamine (via phenylacetyl-CoA:glutamine transferase) → phenylacetylglutamine (excreted). Each mole removes 2 nitrogens (glutamine has 2 N atoms). These 'nitrogen scavenger' drugs divert nitrogen away from urea cycle → reduce hyperammonemia. Used as adjunct therapy in all urea cycle defects. Emergency treatment: IV sodium benzoate + sodium phenylacetate (Ammonul). Reference: Harper's — Nitrogen Scavenger Therapy.

questions.questionText: Glutathione synthesis requires three amino acids in sequence. The rate-limiting step is catalyzed by:
questions.optionB: γ-Glutamylcysteine synthetase (glutamate-cysteine ligase) — combines glutamate and cysteine; cysteine availability is the limiting factor
questions.explanation: Glutathione (GSH, γ-glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine) synthesis: Step 1 (rate-limiting): γ-glutamylcysteine synthetase (glutamate-cysteine ligase): glutamate + cysteine + ATP → γ-glutamylcysteine. CYSTEINE is the limiting substrate (lowest intracellular concentration). Step 2: Glutathione synthetase: γ-glutamylcysteine + glycine + ATP → GSH. Unusual γ-peptide bond between glutamate γ-carboxyl and cysteine α-amino group (makes GSH resistant to peptidases). GSH functions: (1) Antioxidant (reduces H2O2 and organic peroxides via GPx), (2) Drug conjugation (glutathione S-transferase), (3) Amino acid transport (γ-glutamyl cycle), (4) Maintains protein sulfhydryls. N-acetylcysteine (NAC): provides cysteine for GSH replenishment → used in acetaminophen overdose (restores hepatic GSH). Reference: Harper's — Glutathione Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Tyrosinemia type I (hepatorenal tyrosinemia) is caused by deficiency of fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase. The most effective treatment is:
questions.optionB: Nitisinone (NTBC) — inhibits 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase upstream → prevents accumulation of toxic fumarylacetoacetate and succinylacetone
questions.explanation: Tyrosinemia type I: autosomal recessive, fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (FAH) deficiency → last step of tyrosine catabolism. Fumarylacetoacetate (FAA) and maleylacetoacetate accumulate → highly toxic (alkylate DNA, cause oxidative damage). Succinylacetone (pathognomonic metabolite) → inhibits ALA dehydratase → porphyria-like symptoms (elevated ALA). Features: progressive liver failure, hepatocellular carcinoma risk, renal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome), rickets, painful neuropathic crises. Treatment: NITISINONE (NTBC — 2-(2-nitro-4-trifluoromethylbenzoyl)-1,3-cyclohexanedione) → inhibits 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (upstream enzyme) → blocks pathway before toxic intermediates form + dietary tyrosine/phenylalanine restriction. Liver transplant reserved for treatment failures/HCC. Reference: Harper's — Tyrosinemia Type I.

questions.questionText: S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) serves as the universal methyl donor for all of the following methylation reactions EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Thymidylate synthesis (dUMP → dTMP uses N5,N10-methylene-THF, NOT SAM)
questions.explanation: SAM (S-adenosylmethionine) methylation reactions — common NEET PG trap: SAM is the methyl donor for >100 methyltransferase reactions including: DNA methylation (CpG sites, by DNMTs), RNA methylation, histone methylation, norepinephrine → epinephrine (PNMT), PE → PC (PEMT — 3 sequential methylations), guanidinoacetate → creatine, acetyl serotonin → melatonin, phospholipid methylation, drug/xenobiotic methylation. EXCEPTION: Thymidylate synthesis (dUMP → dTMP) uses N5,N10-METHYLENE-THF (from folate cycle) as the one-carbon donor, NOT SAM. The thymidylate synthase reaction is unique — the folate cofactor donates both the one-carbon unit AND reducing equivalents. SAM → S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH) after methyl transfer → adenosine + homocysteine. Reference: Harper's — SAM Methylation Reactions.
